name: Michigan
tooltip: Michigan — Cat 4: No codified statutory right of publicity. Common-law misappropriation may apply.
rightOfPublicity: Common law only. Michigan has not enacted a codified general right-of-publicity statute. Common-law misappropriation may apply — elements vary, but generally: defendant appropriated plaintiff's identity for commercial value, plaintiff is identifiable, defendant received a benefit. Consult an attorney for state-specific application.
postMortem: No codified post-mortem term. Descendibility uncertain at common law.
aiCoverage: Mich. Pub. Acts 263–266 of 2023 (election deepfakes; MCL 168.932f). HBs 4047/4048 of 2025 (AI-NCII).
remedy: Common-law misappropriation suit. Federal claims available regardless: Lanham Act § 43(a) false endorsement, defamation, and federal NCII statutes apply nationwide.
whatsInteresting: Michigan hasn't built statutory likeness protection. AI-specific statutes exist for narrow areas (election deepfakes, NCII, etc.) but the general right-of-publicity base is common law only.
primarySources: Common-law cases vary by state; consult an attorney licensed in Michigan.
Federal statutes and any state AI-specific statutes listed above.
